name: Pump.Science (URO)
one_liner: Companion gamified longevity token to $RIF within the Pump.Science system, trading at ~$0.002 after the platform's security compromise.
thesis:
URO is the companion token to RIF within the Pump.Science gamified longevity platform. Like RIF, it represents community interest in a specific compound being tested for lifespan extension effects. URO trades at ~$0.002, down 97% from ATH, with a market cap of approximately $2M. Slightly more liquid than the effectively dead RIF token.

The Pump.Science platform's GitHub compromise that exposed private keys equally affects URO's credibility and viability. While URO maintains marginally more market cap than RIF ($2M vs. Effectively $0), both tokens share the same fundamental security breach that undermined platform trust.

The slight price premium of URO over RIF may reflect residual speculative interest or a perception that the underlying compound represented by URO has more scientific merit. However, without clarity on experiment status following the security incident, this differential is likely noise rather than signal.

Like RIF, URO is included for completeness in the DeSci space mapping. The investment case is extremely weak, and any position should be sized for total loss. The broader lesson is that DeSci projects require the same rigorous security standards as DeFi protocols. The 'science' branding does not excuse poor operational security.

name: Pump.Science (RIF)
one_liner: Gamified longevity token that suffered a GitHub compromise exposing private keys, now effectively dead at 99% below ATH.
thesis:
Pump.Science attempted to gamify longevity research by creating tradeable tokens tied to specific compounds being tested for lifespan extension. The $RIF token was one of two companion tokens (alongside $URO) that represented community interest in specific research outcomes. The concept was novel, turning scientific experiments into speculative markets where token prices would reflect community conviction about compound efficacy.

The project suffered a catastrophic GitHub compromise that exposed private keys, fundamentally undermining trust in the platform's security. RIF trades at effectively $0, down 99%+ from ATH, with experiment status unknown following the security incident. This represents a cautionary tale about the intersection of DeFi security and science funding.

The gamification model itself was new and attracted attention in the DeSci space, but the execution failure was severe. Security incidents in DeSci carry outsized reputational damage because the sector depends on trust. Researchers and patients need to believe their data, funds, and IP are safe. A private key exposure is the worst possible failure mode.

Included for completeness and as a risk case study. The investment case is functionally zero at current levels. Any recovery would require a complete platform rebuild with audited security, which has not been announced.

Meta Materials Inc. Is effectively bankrupt with a market cap of approximately $700. Not just patents and press releases.

There is no credible bull case for recovery. The company lacks the cash, talent, and commercial relationships needed to rebuild. Avoid completely.

name: Bolt Threads
one_liner: Dead. Website reads 'no longer operating.' Part of the synbio graveyard that destroyed over $1.1B in investor capital.
thesis:
Bolt Threads is dead. The website reads 'no longer operating.' Along with Amyris and Zymergen, Bolt Threads represents the synbio graveyard. Companies that raised hundreds of millions on the promise of bio-materials but failed to achieve commercial scale.

The failure of Bolt Threads (spider silk materials), Amyris (bio-based chemicals, Chapter 11 with $1.15B debt), and Zymergen (acquired then liquidated) killed at least $1.1B in investor capital and created deep skepticism about the entire bio-materials thesis.

These failures serve as a cautionary tale for the sector and an important reference point when evaluating the surviving synbio companies like Solugen, Pivot Bio, and Galy. The survivors are stronger for having survived the shakeout, but investors must remain vigilant about similar patterns.

No investable action. Equity was wiped. Included for completeness as a graveyard entry.

Amyris filed Chapter 11 in August 2023 with $1.15B in debt and equity was wiped. The company attempted to build a bio-based chemicals and consumer products platform but failed to achieve profitability despite raising enormous amounts of capital.

Amyris was the largest single failure in the synthetic biology graveyard, alongside Bolt Threads (dead) and Zymergen (acquired then liquidated). Together, these three companies killed at least $1.1B in investor capital and created deep skepticism about the entire bio-materials thesis.

The science can work in the lab but the economics of scaling biological production to compete with petroleum-derived chemicals are brutally difficult. The company had real technology but could not bridge the gap between lab-scale and commercial-scale production economics.

name: Ambri
one_liner: DEAD. Filed Chapter 11 May 2024, all assets in liquidation despite $222M raised and Bill Gates backing. A cautionary tale for energy storage.
thesis:
Ambri developed liquid metal battery technology for grid-scale energy storage, raising $222M in venture funding with backing from Bill Gates among other notable investors. The company had secured $1.7B in memoranda of understanding (MOUs), suggesting strong potential market demand for its technology. The liquid metal battery approach promised long-duration storage with minimal degradation.

Despite these promising indicators, Ambri filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in May 2024 and executed a General Assignment on August 21, 2025. All assets are now in liquidation. The company could not bridge the critical gap from prototype to factory. The so-called "valley of death" that has claimed numerous energy storage startups. This failure occurred despite substantial funding, high-profile backers, and a large commercial pipeline.

Ambri serves as the definitive cautionary tale for the energy storage sector. It demonstrates that strong technology, prominent backers, and large pipelines are insufficient without the ability to execute the transition from R&D to manufacturing at scale. Investors in the sector should study Ambri's failure when evaluating similar pre-commercial energy storage companies.

name: DOLO
one_liner: Sprint 21 AVOID: Single counterparty risk. Protocol revenue depends entirely on one entity, existential concentration.
thesis:
DOLO was flagged in the Sprint 20-21 pipeline for verification. The V-agent analysis revealed a fatal structural flaw: the protocol's entire revenue stream depends on a single counterparty.

Single counterparty risk is one of the hardest kills in due diligence because the numbers can look strong. Revenue is real, growth is real, the product works. But when 90%+ of economic activity flows through or depends on one entity, you do not have a protocol, you have a bilateral agreement with extra steps.

If that counterparty stops paying, renegotiates terms, builds internally, or gets acquired, the protocol's revenue goes to zero overnight. No diversification, no fallback, no resilience. This is not a risk that improves with time unless the protocol actively diversifies its counterparty base, which DOLO has shown no evidence of doing.

Verdict: AVOID. Score 20. Not investable until counterparty diversification is demonstrated over 6+ months.

name: FTX / Alameda Research
one_liner: Collapsed crypto exchange and trading firm whose fraud destroyed $8B in customer funds and sent Sam Bankman-Fried to prison.
thesis:
FTX was the second-largest crypto exchange globally before its November 2022 collapse revealed massive fraud. Sam Bankman-Fried was convicted on seven counts of fraud and conspiracy and sentenced to 25 years in prison. Alameda Research, the affiliated trading firm, had been using FTX customer deposits as collateral. A fundamental breach of fiduciary duty.

The FTX collapse destroyed approximately $8B in customer funds and triggered a severe crypto winter. It exposed the dangers of centralized custody, commingled funds, lack of proof of reserves, and regulatory arbitrage through offshore incorporation.

The FTX estate is now in bankruptcy proceedings, with creditors receiving partial recoveries. The FTT token is worthless as a utility token since the exchange no longer operates.

Key lesson for the 8-signal framework. Smart money entry (Sequoia, SoftBank, Ontario Teachers' Pension) failed spectacularly as a signal because the underlying business was fraudulent. Due diligence by even the most sophisticated investors cannot catch deliberate fraud. Proof of reserves and on-chain transparency are essential guardrails.

name: Terra/LUNA
one_liner: Collapsed algorithmic stablecoin that lost $40B in value in May 2022, triggering cascading failures across crypto.
thesis:
Terra/LUNA is the canonical example of algorithmic stablecoin failure. The UST depeg in May 2022 destroyed $40B in value and triggered cascading failures that brought down Three Arrows Capital, Celsius, Voyager, and BlockFi. The 'death spiral' mechanism. Where UST redemptions minted LUNA, crashing LUNA's price, which further undermined UST confidence. Was a design flaw that critics had warned about for years.

Do Kwon was arrested in Montenegro in March 2023 and extradited. The SEC charged Terraform Labs with fraud, and a jury found them liable. The LUNA 2.0 relaunch has failed to gain meaningful traction.

This entry exists as a graveyard reminder. Algorithmic stablecoins without exogenous collateral are fundamentally fragile. Any new project proposing similar mechanisms should be evaluated against the Terra template. The warning signs were clear, including dismissal of critics, reflexive tokenomics, unsustainable yields (Anchor's 20%). Are now canonical red flags.

Key lesson. When the 'working code' is the vulnerability, and the community is an echo chamber rather than a quality filter, the 8-signal framework fails. Community conviction was a contra-indicator, not a positive signal.

name: Lighter
one_liner: The number three ZK perp DEX, up sharply this week to a $534M cap. But that rally sits on a hard truth. Farming revenue fell roughly 70% as monthly perp volume dropped from about $232B to $39B, so LIT now trades near 18-20x sales. A brand new July 2026 buyback-and-burn plus a 6% staking target is the fresh token sink, set against a 50%-of-supply insider cliff that starts vesting in December 2026.
thesis:
Lighter is the number three on-chain perpetual futures exchange. It runs a custom ZK rollup built with Plonky2 STARK and Gnark Plonk circuits that enforce price-time priority at the proof level, real cryptographic work, not a slogan. The code was open-sourced in December 2025 and audited by zkSecurity, who found and helped fix Fiat-Shamir and second-preimage bugs. The cap table is strong. Lighter raised about $89M, with Founders Fund and Ribbit co-leading a $68M round at roughly a $1.5B valuation, alongside Haun, Robinhood Ventures and Dragonfly. Founder Vladimir Novakovski entered Harvard at 16 and was recruited by Ken Griffin to Citadel.

The valuation story has flipped, and the rally is papering over it. At the farming peak, DefiLlama put annualized revenue above $100M, around $10M a month, and the token looked cheap near 4x sales. Then volume collapsed about 83% from the $232B monthly TGE peak to roughly $39B as Season 2 incentives ended, and revenue fell with it to a $26-30M annualized run-rate, about $585K a week lately. Against a $534M market cap after a 15% single-day pop, that is 18-20x sales. LIT isn't a cheap top-three perp DEX anymore. It's a richly priced one whose earnings shrank while its price climbed.

The genuinely new part is the token sink. On July 1, 2026 the team changed tokenomics so buyback purchases now permanently burn LIT, about 15.5M bought back, roughly 6.3% of circulating, and added a 6% staking yield target. Both go straight at the token's weak spot, value accrual. Circle also named USDC the default stablecoin across Lighter's stack in 2026, a credibility win. But that is a stablecoin integration, not the reserve-yield revenue share some coverage implied, and Hyperliquid already struck the bigger USDC-yield-to-buyback deal, so the model isn't unique to Lighter.

The red flags still dominate the near term. Only a few thousand addresses hold LIT, about 5,000 per CoinMarketCap, so demand is thin and reflexive. Justin Sun accumulated 14.89M LIT, roughly 5.32% of circulating, then pulled about $152M from LPs. Daily revenue now runs near $100-120K, down from peak daily fees around $1.3M, and it was already sliding before Sun left. L2BEAT rates the chain Stage 0, a 4/7 multisig can bypass the 21-day upgrade delay instantly, and the sequencer is centralized. The defining event is the December 2026 cliff, when 50% of supply held by team and investors starts vesting. It arrives as a bleed of about 13.9M LIT a month, roughly 5.6% of circulating, not a single 500M dump. The burn has to outrun that bleed.

key_members.name: Vladimir 'Vlad' Novakovski
key_members.background: Entered Harvard at 16. Recruited by Ken Griffin to Citadel. Ex-Quora engineer, ex-Addepar. Co-founded Lunchclub before pivoting to Lighter.
key_members.notable: Built Lighter's ZK architecture and raised about $89M from Founders Fund, Ribbit, Haun, Robinhood Ventures and Dragonfly.

team.team_track_record: Novakovski's pedigree is strong (Citadel, Harvard) but Lighter is his first crypto project. The October 2025 outage and the 83% post-TGE volume collapse are execution concerns, though the ZK engineering itself is real.

competitors.name: Hyperliquid
competitors.comparison: The number one perp DEX with 70%+ of on-chain volume and about $887M TTM revenue. Lighter runs a fraction of its volume.

competitors.name: Aster
competitors.comparison: Formed from the Astherus and APX Finance merger, YZi Labs backed, TGE September 2025. A separate protocol from Aevo, which is the actual Ribbon Finance pivot.

competitors.name: dYdX
competitors.comparison: Down about 96% from its ATH on competition and unlocks. The dYdX Chain migration shipped; only the Ethereum bridge was later closed.

competitors.name: Paradex
competitors.comparison: ZK perp DEX on a Starknet appchain. Its DIME token launched March 2026, so it's no longer pre-token.

competitive.moat: A custom ZK rollup with circuit-level price-time priority, plus app-specific tuning. But Stage 0 centralization still undercuts the trustless claims.
competitive.market_size: On-chain perp DEX volume runs about $100B+/month, and Hyperliquid takes 70%+ of it
competitive.current_penetration: Number three, at roughly $39B/month, well behind number one

verdict:
WATCH, and the math has changed since spring. The old bull anchor, a cheap 4x sales multiple, is gone. Revenue fell roughly 70% to a $26-30M run-rate as farming ended, and a 15% single-day rally to a $534M cap pushed the multiple to about 18-20x. What replaces it is a real value-accrual upgrade, the July 1, 2026 buyback-and-burn plus a 6% staking target. The Circle deal helps on credibility by making USDC the default stablecoin, but it's an integration, not the reserve-yield revenue share some coverage implied, and Hyperliquid already did the bigger version of that.

The near-term risks are heavy and unchanged. A few thousand holders, Stage 0 centralization, and daily revenue down near $100-120K. The defining event is still the December 2026 cliff, when 50% of supply starts vesting at about 13.9M LIT a month, roughly 5.6% of circulating. The thesis now reduces to one race. Can the burn plus real volume recovery outrun that supply bleed. Buy the technology and the new token sink only if you also believe volume comes back. Otherwise the rich multiple and the cliff point the other way. Outcomes here are binary, so size accordingly.

edge_data: The rally hides a valuation trap. Revenue fell about 70% as farming ended while price rose, so the sales multiple went from roughly 4x to 18-20x. LIT got more expensive as the business got smaller.

The Circle deal is a stablecoin integration, not the reserve-yield revenue share some outlets claimed. That USDC-yield-to-buyback model was done first, and at a bigger scale, by Hyperliquid, so Lighter isn't first or unique here.
The December cliff is a monthly bleed, not a one-day dump. About 13.9M LIT a month, roughly 5.6% of circulating, for three years. The real question is whether the new burn can absorb that pace.
Recent weekly revenue is about $585K, near $30M annualized. That's the number the 18-20x multiple has to be judged against, not the old farming-peak figure above $100M.
With only a few thousand holders, price is reflexive. Modest real inflows, or the 15% up day, move it hard in both directions.

thesis_changers.bull_breaks_if: Volume stays near the $39B monthly post-farming base and revenue holds at $26-30M, leaving LIT stranded near 18-20x just as the December cliff starts adding about 5.6% of supply a month.
thesis_changers.bear_breaks_if: The live EVM rollup and Telegram convert into durable organic volume that lifts revenue, while the July buyback-and-burn plus 6% staking sink absorbs the cliff supply, turning LIT into a real cash-flow claim.

value_accrual.mechanism: Essentially all protocol revenue funds open-market LIT buybacks, with a burn leg added July 1, 2026.
value_accrual.note: Recent run-rate passthrough is ~97%, but the full year is nearer 41% since buybacks ramped after TGE, and the burn is brand new with the first burn still pending.
